transcript: The team has special permits to approach humpbacks by boat and even swim close to the whales. Soon after sunrise, the first search begins. The team's photo and video researchers set out on the dawn patrol national geographic photographer flip nicklin records the whales above and below the surface he has no illusions that the whale's world remains largely unknown we're seeing such a small part of what the whale world is it's like trying to study elephants from underwater only when their nose is in the water and then wondering what elephants are doing the rest of their day. Flip's still photos are backed up by moving footage taken by fellow free diver Jason Sturgis. Flip and I pretty much do the documentation. We go out there, be the eyes for what's going on down below. Flip and Jason's mission is to gather background material to enable their colleagues to focus on the whales' behavior. When we come out early in the morning, we try and find whales that are going to be helpful for Megan or Jim. And before they get out here, we try and do the basics. So we try and find them, find out what they're doing uh get a photo id try and take just a tail picture each whale's tail is unique flip and jason are adding to a photo database spanning 30 years to identify returning adults any animal that we run into especially if we can sex it if it's a female with a calf or something that by the situation we can tell you whether it's male or female, is really helpful for us down the road to know who they were, where they were, when they were, all those bits of information. Jason calls in the first situation report to Megan. We are just off of Oluwalu and we are on 2 IDs, GPS, and we're getting to downtime right now. So we should have that by the time you guys get here. All right, man. See you in a bit. Bye. As they carry out their morning sea search, they spot a possible lone male. As it dives, it creates a circle of flat water, a whale footprint. A strong vibration runs through the boat's hull. Is she anywhere else? Whoa, whoa, I got him. I think I got him. I think he's here. They are right above a singing whale. Only male humpbacks sing, and it's thought only during the mating season. The whale appears comfortable with the boat's presence, so Flip takes the chance to observe the singer more closely. As a free diver, he doesn't use equipment that might spook the animal. The whale continues to fill the water with its sun. When you were down there, you just feel this tremendously loud sound. It's almost like being in a kettle drum. It's just the concussion. Less hearing it than feeling it. You're just getting pounded by the sound. It made me hurt. And then going, you know what? Why are they doing this? Why are they doing all this work? Flip's photographs of the whales are the first step. Now, an expert in complex acoustic analysis is needed. Being in the water was great, but Jim is the guy, is the genius to figure out how do we measure this thing? How do we do something that not only we will understand and improve, but other good people in boats and other places where you can't be in the water can have this make sense? Jim Darling has spent three decades deciphering the code of the Hawaiian humpbacks. Initially, researchers could only record life on the surface. Today, an underwater microphone captures communication down below, but still can't penetrate its mysteries whales know the ocean in ways that we just can't possibly imagine and yet it's our job as biologists release that researchers to try to imagine it that's probably what makes it so compelling we're trying to imagine something which is unimaginable in our land-dwelling human context. Jim can single out an individual song from the cacophony below. To sing, whales take a breath on the surface, then dive down about 15 metres. They hang almost motionless, head down so that the sound travels laterally. Air from the lungs vibrates vocal cord tissue that's controlled by a pair of articulated cartilages in the larynx. Looser tissue produces lower notes. The air expands a sac that may resonate the sound and transfer the vibrations to the water. Cycling air between the sac and lungs creates long songs, from From high treble chirrups to low rumbling bass notes. Water's superb sound conductivity transmits the calls at over 1,600 metres per second for up to 160 kilometres. Other whales are blasted by the noise, picking it up with ears similar to ours through tiny slits near the eyes. The mechanism by which whales produce this most complex of all animal songs is now known. Understanding its purpose remains a bigger challenge. In his attempts to decipher the meaning, Jim spends every available hour out with the humpbacks. He can now recognise parts of the song's structure and can even predict what the whales may do next. He's into his buzzers now, so you'd expect him to be coming up any time. Whale song is complex because the animals use a wide variety of notes. They vary the scales, speed and intensity, and even deliberately repeat some parts.
